title: Paraserianthes falcataria
taxonomy: Paraserianthes falcataria is classified as a species within the genus Paraserianthes, which is a part of the pea family Fabaceae. The genus Paraserianthes is also known as Falcataria. This species is native to tropical Asia and is often used as a shade tree in plantations. Paraserianthes falcataria is further classified as a legume tree, due to its ability to fix nitrogen in the soil. Its classification is also reflective of its morphological characteristics, such as its compound leaves and showy flowers.
uses: Paraserianthes falcataria has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. In some parts of the world, its extracts have been used to treat fever, cough, and other respiratory issues. The plant's bark is also believed to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, making it useful in treating skin infections and wounds. Additionally, Paraserianthes falcataria is sometimes used to treat digestive problems, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. However, its effectiveness and safety for human consumption need to be further studied and confirmed.

side_effects: Paraserianthes falcataria, also known as the Burma brown or neem tree, has been reported to cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and eye problems in some individuals who come into contact with its sap or leaves. Ingesting the plant's parts can also lead to gastrointestinal issues, including nausea and diarrhea. The plant's compound, falcarinol, has been shown to be toxic to some animals, causing liver and kidney damage.
